When a light of wavelength λ=9.6×10-7m falls normally on the film from air, then it appears dark when seen normally. questions.questionHtml: A thin oil film of refractive index 1.2 floats on the surface of water (μ=43). When a light of wavelength λ=9.6×10-7m falls normally on the film from air, then it appears dark when seen normally. The minimum change in its thickness for which it will appear bright in normally reflected light by the same light is:
questions.solutionText: Solution Given, Refractive index of the oil, μ oil = 4 3 The wavelength of the light, λ = 9 . 6 × 10 - 7 m When the light will fall on the film, some part of the light will be reflected from the upper surface and some part from the lower surface and rest will be transmitted. The two reflected parts of light will interfere according to their path difference created by the oil film. For normal incidence, the extra path difference created will be given by, ∆ x = 2 d μ oil , where d is the thickness of the oil film. For destructive interference(dark), ∆ x = 2 n - 1 λ 2 . For constructive interference(bright), ∆ x = n λ . For minimum thickness increment for changing dark to bright will be, 2 μ ∆ d = λ 2 . ∆ t = λ 4 μ oil = 9 . 6 × 10 - 7 4 × 1 . 2 = 2 × 10 - 7 m
questions.solution: Solution Given, Refractive index of the oil, μ oil = 4 3 The wavelength of the light, λ = 9 . 6 × 10 - 7 m When the light will fall on the film, some part of the light will be reflected from the upper surface and some part from the lower surface and rest will be transmitted. The two reflected parts of light will interfere according to their path difference created by the oil film. For normal incidence, the extra path difference created will be given by, ∆ x = 2 d μ oil , where d is the thickness of the oil film. For destructive interference(dark), ∆ x = 2 n - 1 λ 2 . For constructive interference(bright), ∆ x = n λ . For minimum thickness increment for changing dark to bright will be, 2 μ ∆ d = λ 2 . ∆ t = λ 4 μ oil = 9 . 6 × 10 - 7 4 × 1 . 2 = 2 × 10 - 7 m
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given,

Refractive index of the oil, μoil=43

The wavelength of the light, λ=9.6×10-7 m

When the light will fall on the film, some part of the light will be reflected from the upper surface and some part from the lower surface and rest will be transmitted.

The two reflected parts of light will interfere according to their path difference created by the oil film.

For normal incidence, the extra path difference created will be given by,

∆x=2dμoil, where d is the thickness of the oil film.

For destructive interference(dark), ∆x=2n-1λ2.

For constructive interference(bright), ∆x=nλ.

For minimum thickness increment for changing dark to bright will be, 2μ∆d=λ2.

∆t=λ4μoil=9.6×10-74×1.2=2×10-7m

questions.solutionText: Solution For two conducting plates placed face to face, the outer surface charges are equal: Q A = Q D = Q 1 + Q 2 2 = 12 + 6 2 = 9 μ C Since total charge on plate 1: Q A + Q B = 12 μ C Q B = 12 − 9 = 3 μ C Since facing surfaces carry equal and opposite charges: Q C = − Q B = − 3 μ C Q C + Q D = − 3 + 9 = 6 μ C The charge distribution on surfaces A , B , C and D are respectively: 9 μ C , 3 μ C , − 3 μ C , 9 μ C `

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For two conducting plates placed face to face, the outer surface charges are equal:

QA=QD=Q1+Q22=12+62=9μC

Since total charge on plate 1: QA+QB=12μC

QB=12−9=3μC

Since facing surfaces carry equal and opposite charges: QC=−QB=−3μC

QC+QD=−3+9=6μC

The charge distribution on surfaces A,B,C and D are respectively:

9μC, 3μC, −3μC, 9μC

questions.solutionText: Solution At a given temperature, molecules of gas may have speeds in a range of zero to infinity. The Maxwell's distribution curve is d N d v vs v curve. N is the total number of molecules of a gas and v is speed of a molecule at a given temperature. The maxima of the curve give the most probable speed of the molecules of gas. Root mean square speed gives a general idea about the speed of a molecule. The area under the Maxwell's curve gives the total number of molecules of the gas and is given as: N = ∫ 0 ∞ d N d v d v !

questions.questionHtml: An object and a plane mirror are shown in figure. Mirror is moved with velocity V as shown. The velocity of image is:
questions.solutionText: Solution Consider the distance of the object at any time t is x from the mirror, then the image will form at same distance, i.e., x . The vertical component of the velocity will be only responsible for the motion of the image since the distance between image and object will change along this direction. If the object is moved a distance h , then the image will shift at height 2 h , therefore, the velocity of the image will be 2 V sinθ .

questions.solutionText: Solution Critical velocity is defined as the velocity at which the gravitational force on the object is equal to the air resistance. questions.solutionText: Solution CO is called π -acid ligand. questions.solutionText: Solution (A) is correct but (R) is incorrect. Due to small size of fluorine, the electronic repulsions in fluorine are higher as compared to chlorine with larger size. questions.solutionText: Solution Gadolinium (Z=64) [Xe] 4 f 7 , 5 d 1 , 6 s 2 Lutetium(Z=71)[Xe] 4 f 14 , 5 d 1 , 6 s 2 Lawrencium(Z=103)[Rn] 5 f 14 , 6 d 1 , 7 s 2 Tantalum(Z=73) [Xe] 4 f 14 , 5 d 3 , 6 s 2 Hence, gadolinium has got incompletely filled f -subshell.
questions.solution: Solution Gadolinium (Z=64) [Xe] 4 f 7 , 5 d 1 , 6 s 2 Lutetium(Z=71)[Xe] 4 f 14 , 5 d 1 , 6 s 2 Lawrencium(Z=103)[Rn] 5 f 14 , 6 d 1 , 7 s 2 Tantalum(Z=73) [Xe] 4 f 14 , 5 d 3 , 6 s 2 Hence, gadolinium has got incompletely filled f -subshell.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Gadolinium (Z=64) [Xe] 4f7,5d1,6s2

Lutetium(Z=71)[Xe] 4f14,5d1,6s2

Lawrencium(Z=103)[Rn] 5f14,6d1,7s2

Tantalum(Z=73) [Xe]4f14,5d3,6s2

Hence, gadolinium has got incompletely filled f-subshell.

questions.solutionText: Solution Prepositions are words that link the nouns, pronouns and phrases to other words in a sentence for the purpose of indicating direction, place, time, relationship etc. 'To' is a preposition that can be used to indicate time, destination or direction, approximate numbers etc. 'To' is also used after nouns, verbs and adjectives. As a preposition, 'to' also indicates the recipient of an action . In option (1), 'His leniency to his subordinates landed him in great difficulties' , 'to' is placed after the abstract noun 'leniency' . The sentence conveys the meaning that, as he was lenient, he faced many problems. Here, 'to' as a preposition indicates the word 'subordinates' , who are the recipients of his lenient action. (2) Incorrect — 'leniency in his subordinates' is not the correct prepositional usage, and 'landed him to great difficulties' is wrong as the correct phrase is 'landed him in difficulties'. (3) Incorrect — 'leniency of his subordinates' changes the meaning, implying the subordinates are lenient. Also, 'landed him to great difficulties' is incorrect. (4) Incorrect — 'leniency for his subordinates' is not the standard prepositional usage with 'leniency'. Also, 'landed him to great difficulties' is incorrect.

questions.solutionText: Solution Complete sentence: If only you had spoken clearly, you would not have been misunderstood. This is a third conditional (past unreal conditional) sentence, which talks about an imaginary situation in the past. The structure is: If + past perfect, subject + would have + past participle Since the subject 'you' is the one being misunderstood (not doing the misunderstanding), the passive voice is required: would have been + past participle (misunderstood) . (1) Incorrect — 'would not be misunderstood' is a second conditional form (present/future unreal), which does not match the past perfect 'had spoken' in the if-clause. (2) Incorrect — 'would not have been misunderstanding' uses the active present participle, which changes the meaning incorrectly. (4) Incorrect — 'would not have misunderstood' is active voice, meaning 'you' did the misunderstanding, which changes the intended meaning of the sentence.

questions.solutionText: Solution Let's place Alice at Position 6. Since she sits opposite David, David must be at Position 3. Bob is immediately to the right of Alice. Because they are facing the center, Alice's right is counter-clockwise. So, Bob is at Position 5. Charlie is immediately to the left of David. Facing the center, David's left is clockwise. So, Charlie sits at Position 4. We have two empty seats left: Position 1 and Position 2. The clues tell us Eve is between Frank and David. Since David is at Position 3, Eve must be next to him at Position 2, which forces Frank into the last remaining seat at Position 1. The Final Arrangement (Clockwise starting from Frank): Frank → Eve → David → Charlie → Bob → Alice. Looking at the arrangement, the person sitting immediately to the left of Alice (clockwise from Position 6) is Frank.

questions.solutionText: Solution Statement: Most of the Indian states existed before independence. Analysis: Conclusion (I): Some Indian states existed before independence. 'Most' means a majority, which is greater than 'some'. If most states existed, then it is certainly true that some states existed. 'Some' is a subset of 'Most'. So conclusion (I) is logically valid . Conclusion (II): All Indian states did not exist before independence. The statement says 'most' existed, which means not all but a majority existed. This implies that some states did not exist before independence. However, conclusion (II) says 'All did not exist' , meaning no Indian state existed before independence. This directly contradicts the given statement. So conclusion (II) is not valid .

questions.solutionText: Solution The 7th day of the month is three days earlier than Friday . Three days earlier than Friday = Tuesday (Friday ← Thursday ← Wednesday ← Tuesday) So, the 7th = Tuesday. From the 7th to the 19th = 19 − 7 = 12 days. 12 days = 1 week + 5 days. So the 19th = Tuesday + 5 days = Sunday (Tuesday → Wednesday → Thursday → Friday → Saturday → Sunday)

The 7th day of the month is three days earlier than Friday.

Find the wrong term in the series:
3,8,15,24,34,48,63
questions.solutionText: Solution Finding the differences between consecutive terms: 8 − 3 = 5 15 − 8 = 7 24 − 15 = 9 34 − 24 = 10 48 − 34 = 14 63 − 48 = 15 The differences are: 5 , 7 , 9 , 10 , 14 , 15 The differences should follow a pattern of consecutive odd numbers: 5 , 7 , 9 , 11 , 13 , 15 The 4th difference is 10 instead of 11 , which means 34 is wrong.
questions.solution: Solution Finding the differences between consecutive terms: 8 − 3 = 5 15 − 8 = 7 24 − 15 = 9 34 − 24 = 10 48 − 34 = 14 63 − 48 = 15 The differences are: 5 , 7 , 9 , 10 , 14 , 15 The differences should follow a pattern of consecutive odd numbers: 5 , 7 , 9 , 11 , 13 , 15 The 4th difference is 10 instead of 11 , which means 34 is wrong.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Finding the differences between consecutive terms:
8−3=5
15−8=7
24−15=9
34−24=10
48−34=14
63−48=15

The differences are: 5,7,9,10,14,15

The differences should follow a pattern of consecutive odd numbers: 5,7,9,11,13,15

The 4th difference is 10 instead of 11, which means 34 is wrong.

questions.questionHtml:
The total number of persons who read magazine X is thrice the number of persons who read only magazine Y. The total number of persons who read magazine Y is thrice the number of persons who read only magazine X. Then, which of the following conclusions can be drawn?

i. The number of persons who read both the magazines is twice the number of persons who read only magazine X.

ii. The total number of persons who read either one magazine or both the magazines is twice the number of persons who read both the magazines.
questions.solutionText: Solution Let a = persons who read only X , b = persons who read both, c = persons who read only Y . Given: a + b = 3 c ... (i) b + c = 3 a ... (ii) From (i): a = 3 c − b Substituting in (ii): b + c = 3 ( 3 c − b ) = 9 c − 3 b 4 b = 8 c b = 2 c From (i): a = 3 c − 2 c = c So a = c and b = 2 c . Statement i: Both magazines = 2 × only X ⇒ b = 2 a ⇒ 2 c = 2 c . True. Statement ii: Either one or both = 2 × both ⇒ a + b + c = 2 b ⇒ c + 2 c + c = 2 ( 2 c ) ⇒ 4 c = 4 c . True.
questions.solution: Solution Let a = persons who read only X , b = persons who read both, c = persons who read only Y . Given: a + b = 3 c ... (i) b + c = 3 a ... (ii) From (i): a = 3 c − b Substituting in (ii): b + c = 3 ( 3 c − b ) = 9 c − 3 b 4 b = 8 c b = 2 c From (i): a = 3 c − 2 c = c So a = c and b = 2 c . Statement i: Both magazines = 2 × only X ⇒ b = 2 a ⇒ 2 c = 2 c . True. Statement ii: Either one or both = 2 × both ⇒ a + b + c = 2 b ⇒ c + 2 c + c = 2 ( 2 c ) ⇒ 4 c = 4 c . True.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let a = persons who read only X, b = persons who read both, c = persons who read only Y.

Given:
a+b=3c ... (i)
b+c=3a ... (ii)

From (i): a=3c−b

Substituting in (ii):
b+c=3(3c−b)=9c−3b
4b=8c
b=2c

From (i): a=3c−2c=c

So a=c and b=2c.

Statement i: Both magazines =2× only X ⇒b=2a⇒2c=2c. True.

Statement ii: Either one or both =2× both ⇒a+b+c=2b⇒c+2c+c=2(2c)⇒4c=4c. True.

questions.solutionText: Solution Given information: - Shyam is the son of Ramesh, but Ramesh is not the mother of Shyam. So Ramesh is the father of Shyam. - Ritu and Ramesh are a married couple. So Ritu is the wife of Ramesh and the mother of their children. - Ram is the brother of Ramesh. So Ram is the uncle (not a child of Ritu). - Neha is the daughter of Ritu. Since Shyam is the son of Ramesh, and Ritu is married to Ramesh, Ritu is the mother of Shyam. Therefore, the son of Ritu is Shyam .

Solution
Given information:
- Shyam is the son of Ramesh, but Ramesh is not the mother of Shyam.
So Ramesh is the father of Shyam.

- Ritu and Ramesh are a married couple.
So Ritu is the wife of Ramesh and the mother of their children.

- Ram is the brother of Ramesh.
So Ram is the uncle (not a child of Ritu).

- Neha is the daughter of Ritu.

Since Shyam is the son of Ramesh, and Ritu is married to Ramesh, Ritu is the mother of Shyam.

Therefore, the son of Ritu is Shyam.

questions.solutionText: Solution Total number of things = n . Repetition is allowed at the time of permutation. When exactly one thing is taken = n (any one of those n things can be taken) When two things are taken = n · n = n 2 and so on. Maximum number of things can be taken = r Hence, number of permutation = n + n 2 + … … + n r

Maximum number of things can be taken=r

This meets the axes at A ( 18 x 1 , 0 ) and B ( 0 , 32 y 1 ) . It is given that slope of the tangent at ( x 1 , y 1 ) is − 4 3 So, − x 1 18 ⋅ 32 y 1 = − 4 3 ⇒ x 1 y 1 = 3 4 ⇒ x 1 3 = y 1 4 = K ∴ x 1 = 3 K and y 1 = 4 K Putting x 1 , y 1 in (i), we get K 2 = 1 ∴ Area of Δ OAB = 1 2 OA . OB = 1 2 ⋅ 18 x 1 ⋅ 32 y 1 = 1 2 ( 18 ) ( 32 ) ( 3 K ) ( 4 K ) = 24 K 2 = 24 sq units ( ∵ K 2 = 1 )

x218+y232=1⇒x1218+y1232=1… (i)

So, −x118⋅32y1=−43
⇒x1y1=34
⇒x13=y14=K

questions.solutionText: Solution The odds against A are 8 : 3 . ∴ P ( A ) = 3 11 Odds against B are 5 : 2 ∴ P ( B ) = 2 7 ∴ P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) = 1 ⇒ 3 11 + 2 7 + P ( C ) = 1 ∴ P ( C ) = 1 − 2 7 − 3 11 ∴ P ( C ) = 34 77 ∴ odds against P ( C ) = 77 − 34 34 = 43 34 But odds against C = 43 17 k ∴ 43 17 k = 43 34 ∴ k = 2
questions.solution: Solution The odds against A are 8 : 3 . ∴ P ( A ) = 3 11 Odds against B are 5 : 2 ∴ P ( B ) = 2 7 ∴ P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) = 1 ⇒ 3 11 + 2 7 + P ( C ) = 1 ∴ P ( C ) = 1 − 2 7 − 3 11 ∴ P ( C ) = 34 77 ∴ odds against P ( C ) = 77 − 34 34 = 43 34 But odds against C = 43 17 k ∴ 43 17 k = 43 34 ∴ k = 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The odds against A are 8:3.
∴P( A)=311
Odds against B are 5:2
∴P(B)=27∴P(A)+P(B)+P(C)=1⇒311+27+P(C)=1∴P(C)=1−27−311∴P(C)=3477
∴ odds against P(C)=77−3434=4334
But odds against C=4317k
∴4317k=4334∴k=2

sin⁡x−sin⁡2x+sin⁡3x=0

sin⁡x⋅cos⁡x(2cos⁡x−1)=0

questions.solutionText: Solution We know that 1 + x 2 n = C 0 + C 1 x 2 + C 2 x 4 + … + C n x 2 n ⇒ x 1 + x 2 n = C 0 x + C 1 x 3 + C 2 x 5 + … + C n x 2 n + 1 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get 1 + x 2 n + 2 n x 2 1 + x 2 n - 1 = C 0 + 3 C 1 x 2 + 5 C 2 x 4 + … + 2 n + 1 C n x 2 n Putting x = 1 , we get n + 1 · 2 n = C 0 + 3 C 1 + 5 C 2 + … + 2 n + 1 C n
questions.solution: Solution We know that 1 + x 2 n = C 0 + C 1 x 2 + C 2 x 4 + … + C n x 2 n ⇒ x 1 + x 2 n = C 0 x + C 1 x 3 + C 2 x 5 + … + C n x 2 n + 1 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get 1 + x 2 n + 2 n x 2 1 + x 2 n - 1 = C 0 + 3 C 1 x 2 + 5 C 2 x 4 + … + 2 n + 1 C n x 2 n Putting x = 1 , we get n + 1 · 2 n = C 0 + 3 C 1 + 5 C 2 + … + 2 n + 1 C n
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

We know that

1+x2n=C0+C1x2+C2x4+…+Cnx2n

⇒x1+x2n=C0x+C1x3+C2x5+…+Cnx2n+1

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

1+x2n+2nx21+x2n-1=C0+3C1x2+5C2x4+…+2n+1Cnx2n

Putting x=1, we get

n+1·2n=C0+3C1+5C2+…+2n+1Cn

questions.solutionText: Solution Let the equation of tangent to the parabola y 2 = x , having slope m is, y = m x + 1 4 m For common tangent to the circle x 2 + y 2 − 6 y + 4 = 0 ∴ Radius 9 − 4 = | 3 − 1 4 m | 1 + m 2 ⇒ 5 ( 1 + m 2 ) = 9 + 1 16 m 2 − 3 2 m ⇒ m = 1 2 So, equation of common tangent is y = 1 2 x + 1 2 ⇒ x − 2 y + 1 = 0 .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let the equation of tangent to the parabola y2=x, having slope m is,
y=mx+14m
For common tangent to the circle
x2+y2−6y+4=0
∴ Radius 9−4=|3−14m|1+m2
⇒5(1+m2)=9+116m2−32m⇒m=12
So, equation of common tangent is
y=12x+12⇒x−2y+1=0.

questions.questionHtml:
Statement-1: The number of common solutions of the trigonometric equations 2sin2⁡θ −cos⁡2θ=0 and 2cos2⁡θ−3sin⁡θ=0 in the interval [0,2π] is two.
Statement-2: The number of solutions of the equation, 2cos2⁡θ−3sin⁡θ=0 in the interval [0,π] is two.
questions.solutionText: Solution 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − cos ⁡ 2 θ = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − ( 1 − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ ) = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 1 + 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ 4 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 1 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = ± 1 2 ∴ θ = π 4 , 3 π 4 , 5 π 4 , 7 π 4 , θ ∈ [ 0 , 2 π ] ∴ θ = π 6 , 5 π 6 , 7 π 6 , 11 π 6 Now 2 cos 2 ⁡ θ − 3 sin ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ 2 ( 1 − sin 2 ⁡ θ ) − 3 sin ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 3 sin ⁡ θ + 2 = 0 ⇒ − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 4 sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ θ + 2 = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − sin ⁡ θ + 4 sin ⁡ θ − 2 = 0 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ ( 2 sin ⁡ θ − 1 ) + 2 ( 2 sin ⁡ θ − 1 ) = 0 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = 1 2 , − 2 But sin ⁡ θ = − 2 , is not possible ∴ sin ⁡ θ = 1 2 , ⇒ θ = π 6 , 5 π 6 Hence, there are two common solution, there each of the statement-1 and 2 are true but statement- 2 is not a correct explanation for statement-1.
questions.solution: Solution 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − cos ⁡ 2 θ = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − ( 1 − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ ) = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 1 + 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ 4 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 1 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = ± 1 2 ∴ θ = π 4 , 3 π 4 , 5 π 4 , 7 π 4 , θ ∈ [ 0 , 2 π ] ∴ θ = π 6 , 5 π 6 , 7 π 6 , 11 π 6 Now 2 cos 2 ⁡ θ − 3 sin ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ 2 ( 1 − sin 2 ⁡ θ ) − 3 sin ⁡ θ = 0 ⇒ − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 3 sin ⁡ θ + 2 = 0 ⇒ − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − 4 sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ θ + 2 = 0 ⇒ 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ − sin ⁡ θ + 4 sin ⁡ θ − 2 = 0 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ ( 2 sin ⁡ θ − 1 ) + 2 ( 2 sin ⁡ θ − 1 ) = 0 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = 1 2 , − 2 But sin ⁡ θ = − 2 , is not possible ∴ sin ⁡ θ = 1 2 , ⇒ θ = π 6 , 5 π 6 Hence, there are two common solution, there each of the statement-1 and 2 are true but statement- 2 is not a correct explanation for statement-1.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
2sin2⁡θ−cos⁡2θ=0
⇒2sin2⁡θ−(1−2sin2⁡θ)=0
⇒2sin2⁡θ−1+2sin2⁡θ=0
⇒4sin2⁡θ=1⇒sin⁡θ=±12
∴θ=π4,3π4,5π4,7π4,θ∈[0,2π]
∴θ=π6,5π6,7π6,11π6
Now 2cos2⁡θ−3sin⁡θ=0⇒2(1−sin2⁡θ)−3sin⁡θ=0⇒−2sin2⁡θ−3sin⁡θ+2=0⇒−2sin2⁡θ−4sin⁡θ+sin⁡θ+2=0⇒2sin2⁡θ−sin⁡θ+4sin⁡θ−2=0⇒sin⁡θ(2sin⁡θ−1)+2(2sin⁡θ−1)=0⇒sin⁡θ=12,−2
But sin⁡θ=−2, is not possible
∴sin⁡θ=12,⇒θ=π6,5π6
Hence, there are two common solution, there each of the statement-1 and 2 are true but statement- 2 is not a correct explanation for statement-1.

questions.questionHtml: The equation of normal to the curve y=(1+x)y+sin-1sin2x) at x=0 is
questions.solutionText: Solution Given curve is y = ( 1 + x ) y + sin - 1 sin 2 x On differentiating w.r.t. x , we get d y d x = ( 1 + x ) y y 1 + x + log ( 1 + x ) d y d x + 2 sin x cos x 1 - sin 4 x ⇒ d y d x ( 0 , 1 ) = 1 [At x = 0 , y = 1 ] Slope of normal at x = 0 is m N = - 1 d y d x ( 0 , 1 ) = - 1 ∴ Equation of normal at 0 , 1 is y - 1 = - 1 ( x - 0 ) ⇒ y - 1 = - x ⇒ x + y = 1
questions.solution: Solution Given curve is y = ( 1 + x ) y + sin - 1 sin 2 x On differentiating w.r.t. x , we get d y d x = ( 1 + x ) y y 1 + x + log ( 1 + x ) d y d x + 2 sin x cos x 1 - sin 4 x ⇒ d y d x ( 0 , 1 ) = 1 [At x = 0 , y = 1 ] Slope of normal at x = 0 is m N = - 1 d y d x ( 0 , 1 ) = - 1 ∴ Equation of normal at 0 , 1 is y - 1 = - 1 ( x - 0 ) ⇒ y - 1 = - x ⇒ x + y = 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given curve is

y=(1+x)y+sin-1sin2x

On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

dydx=(1+x)yy1+x+log(1+x)dydx+2sinxcosx1-sin4x

⇒dydx(0,1)=1 [At x=0, y=1]

Slope of normal at x=0 is

mN=-1dydx(0,1)=-1

∴ Equation of normal at 0,1 is

y-1=-1(x-0)

⇒y-1=-x

⇒x+y=1

questions.solutionText: Solution Given: Maximize Z = 4 x + 5 y subject to 2 x + y ≤ 10 , x + 2 y ≤ 8 , x ≥ 0 , y ≥ 0 Finding the corner points of the feasible region: Point 1: Origin ( 0 , 0 ) : Z = 0 Point 2: ( 5 , 0 ) from 2 x + y = 10 , y = 0 : Z = 20 Point 3: ( 0 , 4 ) from x + 2 y = 8 , x = 0 : Z = 20 Point 4: Intersection of 2 x + y = 10 and x + 2 y = 8 : From first equation: y = 10 − 2 x Substituting: x + 2 ( 10 − 2 x ) = 8 ⇒ x + 20 − 4 x = 8 ⇒ − 3 x = − 12 ⇒ x = 4 y = 10 − 8 = 2 At ( 4 , 2 ) : Z = 4 ( 4 ) + 5 ( 2 ) = 16 + 10 = 26 Maximum value of Z = 26

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given: Maximize Z=4x+5y subject to 2x+y≤10, x+2y≤8, x≥0, y≥0

Finding the corner points of the feasible region:

Point 1: Origin (0,0) : Z=0

Point 2: (5,0) from 2x+y=10, y=0 : Z=20

Point 3: (0,4) from x+2y=8, x=0 : Z=20

Point 4: Intersection of 2x+y=10 and x+2y=8:

From first equation: y=10−2x

Substituting: x+2(10−2x)=8⇒x+20−4x=8⇒−3x=−12⇒x=4
y=10−8=2

At (4,2): Z=4(4)+5(2)=16+10=26

Maximum value of Z=26
